title: Pull request narrative

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Pull request narrative; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GitHub Documentation; accessed 2026-07; section applicable to Pull request narrative
sources.claim: A pull request presents a bounded change for review with linked motivation, implementation summary, risk, tests, screenshots or evidence, and an understandable commit or diff history.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A pull request presents a bounded change for review with linked motivation, implementation summary, risk, tests, screenshots or evidence, and an understandable commit or diff history.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A parser fix PR links the issue, explains the boundary defect, includes the regression test, and identifies no schema or deployment impact. Your goal is to open a focused pull request linked to acceptance criteria and tests. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Remotes and synchronization; Push and non-fast-forward rejection; Actionable issues. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A pull request presents a bounded change for review with linked motivation, implementation summary, risk, tests, screenshots or evidence, and an understandable commit or diff history. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: A pull request is not proof of correctness, approval rules vary, and generated files, migrations, rollout, compatibility, or security may need specialized evidence. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Opening a pull request is only a request to merge, so description, tests, and scope explanation are optional when the diff exists.

includes: Explain what changed, why, validation evidence, risks and follow-up without restating every diff line.
Open a focused pull request linked to acceptance criteria and tests.
excludes: A pull request is not proof of correctness, approval rules vary, and generated files, migrations, rollout, compatibility, or security may need specialized evidence.

outcomes: Open a focused pull request linked to acceptance criteria and tests.

examples.title: Pull request narrative: direct case
examples.situation: A parser fix PR links the issue, explains the boundary defect, includes the regression test, and identifies no schema or deployment impact.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A parser fix PR links the issue, explains the boundary defect, includes the regression test, and identifies no schema or deployment impact.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Treat the PR as a review artifact: make intent, boundaries, evidence, and risk legible so reviewers can evaluate more than raw syntax changes.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Treat the PR as a review artifact: make intent, boundaries, evidence, and risk legible so reviewers can evaluate more than raw syntax changes.

examples.title: Pull request narrative: boundary case
examples.situation: A UI change includes keyboard and narrow-screen evidence plus a note about deliberately unchanged behavior.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A UI change includes keyboard and narrow-screen evidence plus a note about deliberately unchanged behavior.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: A pull request is not proof of correctness, approval rules vary, and generated files, migrations, rollout, compatibility, or security may need specialized evidence.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Opening a pull request is only a request to merge, so description, tests, and scope explanation are optional when the diff exists.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Open a focused pull request linked to acceptance criteria and tests.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Opening a pull request is only a request to merge, so description, tests, and scope explanation are optional when the diff exists.
misconceptions.correction: Treat the PR as a review artifact: make intent, boundaries, evidence, and risk legible so reviewers can evaluate more than raw syntax changes.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Opening a pull request is only a request to merge, so description, tests, and scope explanation are optional when the diff exists.

analogy.example: An editorial desk can model Pull request narrative: a request defines the change, a draft presents evidence, reviewers comment, and approved work enters the edition.
usefulFor: Connecting issues, bounded changes, actionable review, checks, and controlled integration
limitations: Repository permissions, automated checks, security disclosure, and commit identity require explicit technical controls.
A pull request is not proof of correctness, approval rules vary, and generated files, migrations, rollout, compatibility, or security may need specialized evidence.

scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

recap.summary: You should now be able to open a focused pull request linked to acceptance criteria and tests. Keep this boundary in view: A pull request is not proof of correctness, approval rules vary, and generated files, migrations, rollout, compatibility, or security may need specialized evidence.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Pull request narrative without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Opening a pull request is only a request to merge, so description, tests, and scope explanation are optional when the diff exists.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A UI change includes keyboard and narrow-screen evidence plus a note about deliberately unchanged behavior.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Code review”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
